title: Working Capital Management
simple_html:
Working capital is the money a business needs to run its day-to-day operations — cash to pay suppliers and staff while it waits to collect from customers. Formally, it's current assets (cash, inventory, receivables) minus current liabilities (payables, short-term debt). Working capital management is the art of keeping just enough of it: enough to avoid running out of cash, but not so much that money sits idle.

Why it matters

A profitable company can still go bankrupt if it runs out of cash. Working capital management is about liquidity — making sure the firm can always pay its bills on time. Tie up too much cash in inventory and unpaid invoices and you can't fund growth; hold too little and you risk a cash crunch.

The three big levers

  • Inventory — stock waiting to be sold. Less is cheaper, but too little means lost sales.
  • Receivables — money customers owe you. Collect faster to free up cash.
  • Payables — money you owe suppliers. Pay slower (within terms) to keep cash longer.

The cash conversion cycle

The cash conversion cycle (CCC) measures how many days cash is tied up: CCC = days inventory + days receivables − days payables. A shorter cycle means cash returns faster. The goal is to shrink it without harming sales or supplier relationships.

Key takeaways

  • Working capital = current assets − current liabilities.
  • It's about liquidity: paying bills on time, every time.
  • Manage inventory, receivables, and payables together.
  • Shorten the cash conversion cycle to free up cash.
  • Balance the trade-off between liquidity (safety) and profitability (efficiency).
intermediate_html:
Working capital management is the management of a firm's short-term assets and liabilities to ensure it can meet operating obligations while using cash efficiently. It is concerned with liquidity and the smooth functioning of daily operations, complementing the long-term focus of capital budgeting and capital structure.

Key measures

  • Net working capital (NWC) = current assets − current liabilities.
  • Current ratio = current assets ÷ current liabilities (liquidity check).
  • Quick (acid-test) ratio = (current assets − inventory) ÷ current liabilities.

The cash conversion cycle

The CCC is the workhorse metric: CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO, where DIO = Days Inventory Outstanding, DSO = Days Sales Outstanding (receivables), and DPO = Days Payables Outstanding. It tells you how many days elapse between paying for inputs and collecting from customers. The shorter the cycle, the less external financing the firm needs to fund operations.

Managing the components

  • Inventory: Use models like the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) to minimize ordering + holding costs, or just-in-time (JIT) to slash holding costs. The trade-off: lower inventory frees cash but risks stockouts.
  • Receivables: Set credit policy (terms, screening), and use discounts (e.g., "2/10 net 30") to speed collection. Faster collection lowers DSO and financing costs but tighter credit may lose sales.
  • Payables: Take full credit terms suppliers offer, but evaluate early-payment discounts — forgoing "2/10 net 30" has a high implied annual cost (~37%).
  • Cash: Hold enough for transactions and precaution; invest surpluses in liquid securities.

Financing strategies

Firms choose how to finance current assets: a conservative policy uses long-term funds for most working capital (safe but costly), an aggressive policy relies on short-term debt (cheaper but riskier), and a matching (hedging) policy funds permanent working capital with long-term sources and temporary needs with short-term sources.

Worked example

A firm has DIO = 60 days, DSO = 45 days, DPO = 30 days.

CCC = 60 + 45 − 30 = 75 days. The firm waits 75 days from cash outlay to cash recovery, which it must finance. If it negotiates suppliers to DPO = 50 and tightens collections to DSO = 35, CCC falls to 60 + 35 − 50 = 45 days — freeing significant cash. If daily operating outlay is $100,000, cutting the cycle by 30 days releases roughly $3m of tied-up cash.

How to apply it

  • Benchmark DIO, DSO, DPO against industry peers to spot inefficiency.
  • Attack the largest component first; small day reductions on big balances free the most cash.
  • Evaluate trade discounts using the implied annual rate before forgoing them.
  • Match financing to the permanence of the asset — don't fund permanent inventory with overnight debt.
full_html:
Working capital management concerns the administration of a firm's current assets (cash, marketable securities, accounts receivable, inventory) and current liabilities (accounts payable, accruals, short-term debt) to sustain operations while balancing liquidity against profitability. Whereas capital budgeting (Lesson 4) and capital structure (Lesson 7) address long-horizon decisions, working capital management governs the firm's short-term financial health — and it is here that otherwise profitable firms most often fail, because profit is an opinion but cash is a fact: insolvency is a cash problem, not an earnings problem.

Core concepts and metrics

Net working capital = current assets − current liabilities measures the buffer of liquid resources. Liquidity is gauged by the current ratio (CA/CL) and the more stringent quick ratio, which excludes inventory. The central operational metric is the cash conversion cycle (Richards & Laughlin, 1980):

CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO

where DIO = (Inventory/COGS)×365, DSO = (Receivables/Sales)×365, and DPO = (Payables/COGS)×365. The CCC measures the number of days that cash is locked in the operating cycle and therefore must be financed. Reducing it releases cash and lowers financing costs; some firms (e.g., subscription or large-retail models) achieve a negative CCC, effectively financing operations with suppliers' and customers' money.

The liquidity–profitability trade-off

The fundamental tension is that liquidity is safe but costly. Holding large cash balances, generous inventory, and lax credit reduces the risk of stockouts and default but lowers return on assets because those resources earn little. Conversely, lean working capital boosts returns but raises the risk of operational disruption and technical default. Optimal management finds the point where the marginal benefit of additional liquidity equals its marginal cost — a balance that varies by industry, seasonality, and the firm's access to financing.

Managing the components

Inventory. The classic Economic Order Quantity model (Harris, 1913; Wilson) minimizes the sum of ordering and holding costs: EOQ = √(2DS/H), where D is annual demand, S the order cost, and H the per-unit holding cost. Just-in-time (JIT) systems and vendor-managed inventory push holding costs toward zero but increase exposure to supply-chain disruption — a trade-off vividly exposed during recent global supply shocks.

Receivables. Credit policy spans the standards for granting credit, the terms offered, and collection effort. Trade discounts such as "2/10 net 30" accelerate collection; the firm balances the cost of the discount and bad-debt risk against the financing savings of faster DSO. Aging schedules and the average collection period monitor performance.

Payables. Trade credit is often "free" within the net period, so firms should take the full term. But forgoing an early-payment discount is expensive: declining "2/10 net 30" implies an annualized cost of roughly (0.02/0.98) × (365/20) ≈ 37.2% — far above most borrowing rates, so such discounts should usually be taken.

Cash. The Baumol (1952) and Miller–Orr (1966) models formalize the demand for cash, trading off the opportunity cost of idle balances against the transaction cost of converting securities to cash. Surplus cash should be parked in highly liquid, low-risk instruments.

Working-capital financing strategies

Current assets split into a permanent component (the minimum always required) and a temporary, seasonal component. Under the matching (hedging) principle, permanent working capital is financed with long-term sources and temporary needs with short-term debt, aligning asset and liability maturities. A conservative strategy over-funds with long-term capital (lower risk, lower return); an aggressive strategy leans on short-term debt (higher return, higher refinancing and interest-rate risk). The choice reflects management's risk appetite and the firm's access to capital markets.

Worked example

A firm reports DIO = 60, DSO = 45, DPO = 30, giving CCC = 60 + 45 − 30 = 75 days. Daily operating cash outlay is $100,000, so roughly $7.5m is tied up in the cycle. Management negotiates longer supplier terms (DPO → 50) and tightens collections (DSO → 35): CCC = 60 + 35 − 50 = 45 days, releasing about $3.0m of cash — funds that can repay short-term debt, lowering interest expense, or finance growth without new external capital. This illustrates how working-capital efficiency directly affects the firm's financing needs and its WACC (Lesson 6).

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Equating profit with cash. A growing, profitable firm can suffer a cash crunch as receivables and inventory swell faster than collections — "overtrading."
  • Chasing a high current ratio. Excess liquidity signals idle, under-deployed assets; very high ratios can indicate poor efficiency, not strength.
  • Forgoing trade discounts blindly. The implied annual cost is usually far above borrowing rates.
  • Maturity mismatch. Funding permanent assets with short-term debt invites a refinancing crisis when credit tightens.
  • Optimizing components in isolation. Squeezing one party (e.g., delaying payables aggressively) can damage supplier relationships and ultimately raise input costs.

Cross-links

Working capital management connects to Financial Statement & Ratio Analysis (Lesson 3) through liquidity and activity ratios, to Cost of Capital & WACC (Lesson 6) because freed cash reduces financing needs, and to Time Value of Money (Lesson 2) in evaluating discount and credit decisions. As the operational counterpart to the firm's long-term financial strategy, it ensures the business survives day to day so the value created by good investment and financing decisions can actually be realized (Lesson 1).
